concept.title: Rule set governance and versioning
concept.shortdesc: A rule set is a living document. Good governance practices protect your authors from unexpected pipeline failures while allowing your editorial standards to evolve over time.

othermeta.name: description
othermeta.content: Best practices for managing markdown-validator rule sets: versioning, communicating changes to authors, when to use Required vs. Suggested, and deprecating rules.

section.title: Store the rule set in version control
section.p: Commit rule set files to the repository alongside the content they govern, typically under rules/. This keeps author and CI rule sets in sync and makes rollback a simple revert.

section.title: Deciding between Required and Suggested
section.p: The level of a rule determines whether authors are blocked or merely informed. Use the following criteria:

row.entry: The check enforces a hard contractual requirement (legal, accessibility, platform dependency)
The check enforces a well-established style rule that all authors have been trained on
You are introducing a new rule and want to monitor compliance before enforcing it
The check is a recommendation that should be visible but should not block publication

section.note: Introduce new Required rules gradually. Start as Suggested, run reports to see how many articles fail, communicate the upcoming change to your authors, then promote to Required after the team has had time to address the backlog.

section.title: Communicating rule changes to authors
section.p: Authors who are blocked by a new rule they did not expect are frustrated. Follow this process when adding or promoting a rule:

ol.li: Run a report across the repository to identify affected articles and their owners.
Notify authors of the upcoming change with enough lead time to fix their articles (at least two weeks for a large repository).
After the compliance window, change the level to Required and commit the update.

section.title: Deprecating and removing rules
section.p: When a rule no longer reflects your editorial standards:

ol.li: Change the level from Required to Suggested to stop blocking authors while you plan the removal.
Remove the rule from the rule set file.

section.p: Do not change a rule's ID once it is in use. If you change the rule significantly, treat it as a new rule with a new ID and deprecate the old one.

section.title: Maintain a changelog
section.p: Keep a CHANGELOG.md alongside your rule set file to document what changed, when, and why. Authors who receive unexpected failures will search for this file first. At minimum, record:

ul.li: Which rules were added, changed, or removed
Whether a Suggested rule was promoted to Required
